summary:
The Wounded Knee Massacre on 29 December 1890, at the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation in South Dakota, was the killing of approximately 250–300 Lakota Sioux men, women, and children by the U.S. Army's 7th Cavalry Regiment. It is widely regarded as the end of the Indian Wars and the final major armed confrontation between the United States government and Native American peoples.

The massacre occurred in the context of the Ghost Dance movement — a new religious practice spreading among Great Plains tribes that combined elements of Christianity and Native spirituality, promising the end of white domination and the return of the buffalo. The Ghost Dance alarmed the U.S. government, which feared it as a precursor to armed uprising. Sitting Bull, the famous Hunkpapa Lakota leader, was killed during his arrest on 15 December 1890 in a fracas at Standing Rock.

A band of Miniconjou Lakota under Chief Big Foot, many of them Ghost Dancers, was intercepted and escorted to Wounded Knee Creek. On the morning of 29 December, while soldiers were attempting to disarm the Lakota camp, a shot was fired — its origin disputed. The soldiers immediately opened fire with rifles and Hotchkiss mountain guns. Within minutes approximately 250 Lakota were dead, including Big Foot himself; soldiers suffered 25 deaths, many from friendly fire. The dead Lakota were buried in a mass grave.

Wounded Knee became a symbol of the long history of dispossession and violence against Native Americans. Dee Brown's book Bury My Heart at Wounded Knee (1970) brought the massacre and the broader history of American Indian policy to wide public attention. In 1973 the site was occupied by members of the American Indian Movement in a 71-day standoff with federal authorities, drawing renewed attention to Native American grievances.
